chunk_summary: This section outlines a three-layer Data Quality Assurance (DQA) framework for pediatric nutrition programs, emphasizing automated plausibility checks, field verification, and triangulation to ensure accurate monitoring and corrective actions.
learning_objectives: Understand the three-layer DQA framework for pediatric nutrition data quality assurance.
Learn to identify and address biologically implausible data and misclassification errors.
Apply triangulation techniques to reconcile discrepancies in nutrition program data.
Develop corrective actions based on DQA findings to improve program outcomes.
key_facts: DQA involves three layers: automated plausibility screening, field verification, and triangulation.
Field verification recommends re-measuring 5–10% of children monthly using calibrated tools.
Automated plausibility checks flag biologically implausible z-scores, sudden shifts in wasting/stunting, and impossible denominators (e.g., >100% coverage).
Triangulation reconciles tracker reports with stock registers, attendance records, and caregiver recall.
Abrupt indicator improvements after training or device rollouts may signal data quality issues rather than true impact.
Corrective actions include refresher training for measurement errors and activating grievance systems for service delivery gaps.
reference_quote: Routine nutrition dashboards are only as credible as their data quality assurance, so clinicians should learn basic DQA rules that convert 'numbers' into actionable evidence.
document_key_topics: Data Quality Assurance in Pediatric Nutrition
Corrective Actions for Nutrition Program Monitoring
